medications.drug_name: additional dose of oxycodone or other opioid
medications.indication_rationale: Pain refractory to a full dose of strong opioid is itself a red flag; simply increasing the dose masks the warning sign and dangerously delays the cast removal that is actually needed.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the leg level with the body (heart height) rather than elevated high, and avoid any weight-bearing or attempts to move the toes.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Elevating the leg above heart level reduces arterial inflow pressure, which can worsen tissue ischaemia in compartment syndrome; keeping it level preserves perfusion.

patient_education_points.point: Your pain not responding to the strong painkiller, plus the pain when moving your toes and the pins and needles, are warning signs that the pressure inside your leg under the cast is dangerously high and cutting off blood flow to the muscles and nerves.
Call 000 for an ambulance right now — you need the cast cut open and your leg assessed urgently in hospital, likely with emergency surgery to release the pressure.
Keep your leg flat, level with your body — don't prop it up high, as that can make things worse.
Try to keep your toes as still as possible until help arrives, as movement is causing severe pain.

follow_up_plan.interval: Immediate emergency transport for urgent cast removal and orthopaedic assessment
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Intracompartmental pressures, need for fasciotomy, neurovascular status of the limb, and long-term functional outcome
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Not applicable — already an emergency requiring immediate transport

must_include_items: Explicit recognition that opioid-refractory pain is a red flag, not a reason for more analgesia
Advice to keep the leg level, not elevated high
Recognition that a palpable pulse does not rule out compartment syndrome
errors_of_omission: No explicit warning against simply increasing analgesia
errors_of_commission: Recommending an additional dose of opioid as the primary management
Advising the leg be elevated high
Reassuring the patient/parent based on a palpable pulse that this is not an emergency
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Recommending increased analgesia instead of emergency cast removal, or using a palpable pulse for reassurance, are critical safety failures given the limb-threatening nature of this condition.
